text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old male with known Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutant, MSS) with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. Undergoing restaging following 6 cycles of mFOLFOX6 plus Avastin. Evaluate for treatment response. CEA trending downward (most recent 54.2 ng/mL, down from 187.4 ng/mL at diagnosis).

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast obtained at initial staging (approximately 3 months prior).

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed in the portal venous phase following administration of 120 mL of iohexol (Omnipaque 350) intravenous contrast. Delayed hepatic phase acquisitions were obtained through the liver. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformatted images were reviewed in conjunction with axial acquisitions. No oral contrast administered per departmental protocol for oncologic restaging.

---

FINDINGS:

LIVER:
All four previously identified hepatic metastases demonstrate substantial interval decrease in size and conspicuity, indicative of a marked treatment response.

Segment VI: The dominant lesion in the right hepatic lobe, previously measuring 3.8 x 3.1 cm on the prior staging examination, now measures approximately 2.1 x 1.7 cm. The lesion retains its hypodense appearance on portal venous phase imaging with subtle peripheral rim hypoenhancement. No internal hemorrhage or calcification. The overall volume of viable tumor in this segment appears appreciably reduced. The lesion remains clear of the right hepatic vein by greater than 1 cm. Relationship to the right portal vein is unchanged; no direct invasion.

Segment II: The previously identified left lobe lesion, previously measuring 1.9 x 1.6 cm, now measures approximately 0.9 x 0.7 cm. This lesion appears as a faint low-density focus on the current examination and is only confidently identified on direct comparison with prior imaging. It lies in close proximity to the left hepatic vein but does not appear to directly abut the vessel wall on current images.

Segment IV: The medial left lobe lesion, previously measuring 2.2 x 1.8 cm, now measures approximately 1.1 x 0.9 cm. Peripheral hypoenhancement persists but internal density appears more homogeneous than on prior examination, possibly reflecting treatment-induced necrosis or fibrotic replacement. No new satellite lesions in this segment.

Segment VII: The small lesion previously identified measuring 1.1 x 0.9 cm in segment VII is now reduced to approximately 0.5 x 0.4 cm. At this small size, characterization is limited; the residual focus is subcentimeter and hypodense on all phases. Near-complete treatment response in this lesion cannot be excluded.

No new hepatic lesions are identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein and hepatic veins patent without thrombosis. No hepatic capsular contour abnormality. No perihepatic free fluid.

GALLBLADDER AND BILIARY SYSTEM:
Gallbladder is present with no evidence of cholelithiasis or wall thickening. Common bile duct measures 4 mm in diameter, within normal limits. No pneumobilia.

PRIMARY COLONIC TUMOR (SIGMOID COLON):
The previously identified asymmetric wall thickening and mass-like lesion of the mid-sigmoid colon has decreased in size and mural infiltrative extent. The lesion now measures approximately 3.2 x 2.6 cm in maximal cross-sectional dimension (previously 4.5 x 3.8 cm). The degree of pericolonic fat stranding and tethering of adjacent mesenteric soft tissue has noticeably diminished compared to the prior study, though residual desmoplastic reaction remains. The mass retains irregular mural thickening extending toward the mesenteric fat. There is no evidence of acute perforation. No upstream colonic dilatation to suggest obstruction. The perirectal/pericolonic fat planes have modestly improved. The lesion does not appear to involve the posterior pelvic sidewall.

MESENTERIC AND RETROPERITONEAL LYMPH NODES:
Several pericolonic lymph nodes were noted on prior imaging, the largest measuring 1.4 cm. On current examination, these nodes appear smaller; the previously dominant node is now estimated at approximately 0.9 cm in short axis. No new enlarged lymphadenopathy is identified in the mesentery, retroperitoneum, or pelvis. No paraaortic adenopathy. Gastrohepatic ligament nodes not enlarged.

SPLEEN:
Unremarkable. Normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

PANCREAS:
Normal caliber and morphology throughout the head, body, and tail. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic fluid or stranding.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are slender and unremarkable. No adrenal nodules or masses.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS:
Both kidneys are of normal size and demonstrate symmetric cortical enhancement without focal mass. No hydronephrosis. No nephrolithiasis. No perinephric stranding. Bilateral renal cortical thinning is not present.

URINARY BLADDER:
Bladder is adequately distended with a smooth wall contour. No intraluminal filling defects or mural thickening.

PELVIS:
The rectum and distal sigmoid are within normal limits below the level of the primary tumor. No pelvic free fluid. No pelvic sidewall adenopathy. Prostate is unremarkable in size and attenuation.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
The peritoneal surface and mesenteric fat are carefully evaluated. No peritoneal implants, omental cake, or ascites are identified. No evidence of carcinomatosis. The small bowel loops are normally opacified and without wall thickening or obstruction.

VASCULATURE:
The aorta is of normal caliber throughout the abdomen and into the pelvis, without evidence of aneurysmal dilatation. Celiac, superior mesenteric, and inferior mesenteric arterial origins are patent. No mesenteric venous thrombosis. Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the aortoiliac system, appropriate for patient's age.

BONE WINDOWS:
Visualized portions of the lumbar spine, pelvis, femoral heads, and lower ribs are examined. No lytic or blastic osseous lesions to suggest skeletal metastatic involvement. Mild degenerative facet joint changes at L4-L5 and L5-S1. No pathologic fractures.

SOFT TISSUES:
No abnormal soft tissue masses in the abdominal or pelvic walls. Postsurgical changes are not present. Small volume subcutaneous fat is uniform in appearance.

CHEST (not formally included in current examination; no CT chest obtained at this restaging):
The lung bases are included on the upper abdominal images. The visualized lung bases are clear. No pleural effusions. No evidence of subpleural nodules at the lung bases bilaterally on the limited axial views available.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. MARKED INTERVAL DECREASE IN SIZE OF ALL FOUR HEPATIC METASTASES, consistent with a substantial and ongoing treatment response to mFOLFOX6 and bev therapy. The dominant segment VI lesion has decreased from 3.8 cm to 2.1 cm, the segment II lesion from 1.9 cm to 0.9 cm, the segment IV lesion from 2.2 cm to 1.1 cm, and the segment VII lesion from 1.1 cm to 0.5 cm. No new hepatic lesions are identified.

2. INTERVAL DECREASE IN SIZE OF THE PRIMARY SIGMOID COLON MASS, now measuring approximately 3.2 x 2.6 cm compared to 4.5 x 3.8 cm previously. Reduced pericolonic inflammatory change and improved mesenteric fat planes. No evidence of bowel obstruction.

3. INTERVAL DECREASE IN PERICOLONIC LYMPH NODE SIZE, with the previously dominant node reducing from 1.4 cm to approximately 0.9 cm in short axis. No new regional adenopathy.

4. NO NEW SITES OF DISEASE. No peritoneal implants, pulmonary nodules on limited views, osseous metastases, or adrenal lesions identified.

5. The overall hepatic tumor burden, while still present, has decreased substantially. The segment II lesion's proximity to the left hepatic vein remains a technical consideration; however, the continued size reduction suggests that hepatic resection candidacy may warrant reassessment by the hepatobiliary surgical team. Clinical correlation and multidisciplinary review are suggested.

Overall, these findings are in keeping with a meaningful favorable treatment response across all sites of known disease.

Electronically signed by: Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging Division
